[PDF_PAGE 107 | score=3] Fiendflower Terrain: Deep forest or jungle Search TielModifier: 12/-4 Preparation TimelModifier: 8/-3 Application: Tea Effects: Characters who drink this bitter tea see horrifying visions that drive them tem- porarily mad. After ld20 turns, drinkers suf- $er an episode of 2d6 minutes, during which .hey turn enraged, violent, and homicidal; ;uch a PC should be played by the DM. After- ward, drinkers cannot recall what happened luring the episode, which recurs once per lay (at irregular intervals) for the next 2d4 days. A neutralize poison spell ends fiend- ~owei's effects. [PDF_PAGE 116 | score=3] I 1 Scrying pool. A source of still water within the grove, such as a pool or well, may be used for divination. Once per day a druid can com- mand the pool or well to act as a r+'ecting pool st at the druid's level. 1 Magic Fruit. The grove has a tree whose f it or berries have a magical effect when e ten. In a given year, ld6 fruits ripen, each 'th a distinct appearance to set it apart from c 4 mmon fruit-lustrous golden apples, for example. The magic fruit's effects are equiva- lent to one of the following: Potion of animal control Potion of heroism Potion aftreasureflnding Philter of love Philter ofglibness. Forbiddance. [PDF_PAGE 124 | score=3] . . ... ). .'I "' . j. :' . . . . . .. . . . , \st-kvd spek Animalfriendship, ceremony, defect balance, detect mgic, defecf poison, detect snares and pits, entangle,faeriefire, in*ibility to animals, locate animals, pass without trace, pre- cipitation, predict weather, purifY water, shille- lagh, speak with animals. 2nd-level spells: Barkskin, charm person or mammal, create water, cure light wounds, feign death,@ trap,flame blade, goodberry, heat metal, locate plants, obscurement, produceflame, re&- ing pool, slow poison, h.p, warp wood. 3rd-level spells: Call lightning, cloudburst, cure disease, hold animal, know alignment, neu- tralize poison, plant growth, protectionfromfire, pyrotechnics
[PDF_PAGE 9 | score=1] 30%~15 hp, or 4.5 hp. This value becomes 4 hit points after rounding. The animal form a druid assumes can vary from the size of a bullfrog or small bird to that of a black bear. Unless noted otherwise, the druid can assume only the form of a normal (real-world) animal in normal proportions. A druid in animal form takes on all the beast's physical characteristics- movement rate, abilities, Armor Class (AC), number of attacks, and damage per attack. The druid retains original hit point and saving throw values. The druid's clothing and one item held in each hand also become part of the new body; these reappear when the druid resumes nor- mal shape. Generally, a druid in animal form cannot use s
[PDF_PAGE 22 | score=1] Fighbleddruids. Often acting as wandering guardidns of Nature and country folk (much like neqtral rangers), fighter/druids also can love Nature, and decide to adopt a ne become hermit-knighis, living away from society and defending a particular grove with their lives. Fighters who become druids often do so because they seek spiritual growth, because they have grown disgusted with the world of man, or occasionally as penance for a particular misdeed. Druids who become fighters, on the other hand, want to take a more direct approach to defending the wilderness; others seek to attune themselves to Nature by mastering their own bodies using eastern-style fighting arts, often becoming rather enig
[PDF_PAGE 39 | score=1] lruids in other branches have limits on their shapechanging powers. Weapon Proficiencies: Recommended- Secondary Skills: Hunter, groom. Nonweapon Proficiencies: Bonus- animal Recommended-(priest) spellcraft; (war- endurance, survival, tracking. uipment: The druid should spend her 1 allotment of gold pieces entirely on equipment, as she loses any unspent starting money in excess of 1 gp. Special Benefits: As a Shapeshifter, Rimi gains her branch’s shapechanging power at 1st level rather than at 7th level. However, until she reaches 7th level, the druid can assume only the form of natural creatures whose Hit Dice total no greater than half her level. (A lst- level Shapeshifter only assumes the
[PDF_PAGE 47 | score=1] magical--throughout the domain. The Moot. The three archdruids share the druid’s responsibility for initiating newcom- ers to the Order. In addition, they each have the right to summon a moot: a gathering of the entire circle, traditionally held at the sol- stices and equinoxes. By ancient custom, moots are called four times a year at these set dates, once by each archdruid and once (usu- ally in spring) by the great druid. A moot on a nontraditional date means the summoner sees something so deeply amiss in the domain that the entire circle must discuss it as soon as possible. These gatherings enable the circle to cele- brate the changing of the seasons, to gossip and socialize, to exchange 
[PDF_PAGE 53 | score=1] ceep tHe Grand Druid informed on the opera- tions of the circles in every land, they roam he world, visiting the various circles as well 1s other places of interest to their master. The mivallof an Emissary often coincides with he asckndance of a new great druid. While ?onvey’ing the respects of the head of the 3rder, the archdruid takes the new leader’s neasure and reports back to the Grand Druid. rhese servants also visit a circle in response LO a great druid‘s request for aid. Traveling Emissaries normally find them- ;elves welcomed, for their visits give circles a .hance to learn news from far-off lands. An Emissaty also may offer counsel about a men- ace or carry a request for help to t
